question.content: A quarter-circle curved bar AB has radius R and a solid circular cross section of diameter d with d << R. End A is fixed, end B is free, and a concentrated force F acts at B perpendicular to the plane of the bar axis. Given the allowable tensile stress [sigma], determine the allowable load [F] using the third strength theory. Answer format: a single algebraic expression for [F]; write only the expression, with no explanatory text or equals sign.

step_1.content: Section-force vector / principal-stress method: cut the bar at section A, read the internal resultant vector there, then determine the principal stress state before applying the third strength theory.
step_1.key_point: This route goes through the stress-state interpretation instead of writing the equivalent-stress formula immediately.

step_3.content: At section A, the end force F produces a bending resultant and a torsional resultant with the same arm R. So the critical surface point is under a combined normal-plus-shear stress state generated by M and T together.
step_3.key_point: The dangerous point is identified first through the section-resultant picture, not through a direct substitution into a ready-made formula.

step_5.content: Using the section properties of the solid circular bar gives the surface bending stress and torsional shear stress. From that plane-stress state, obtain the principal stresses and then use the third strength theory, which compares the maximum shear-related intensity with the allowable tensile stress [σ].
step_5.key_point: The final allowable load comes from the principal-stress interpretation of the combined state, which is a different solving route from solution1.
